one_line_takeaway: Injecting as few as 5 attacker-crafted texts per question into a million-text RAG knowledge base achieves ~90-99% attack success across 8 LLMs by jointly satisfying a retrieval and a generation condition, and common defenses (paraphrasing, perplexity, dedup, larger k) fail to stop it.
key_finding: RAG is highly vulnerable: injecting just 5 malicious texts per target question into databases of millions of clean texts yields ~90%+ ASR. On NQ with PaLM 2, black-box ASR is 0.97 and white-box 0.97; on HotpotQA 0.99 (black-box); on MS-MARCO 0.91 (black-box). High ASRs hold across all 8 LLMs (e.g., NQ black-box: GPT-4 0.97, GPT-3.5 0.92, LLaMA-2-7B 0.97, Vicuna-7B 0.88). PoisonedRAG strongly outperforms adapted baselines (NQ default): PoisonedRAG 0.97 vs Prompt Injection 0.62, Disinformation 0.69, Naive 0.03, Corpus Poisoning 0.01, GCG 0.02 - the failures pinpoint that baselines miss either the retrieval condition (GCG, ASR 0.02) or the generation condition (Corpus Poisoning/Naive). The attack is cheap: ~2 GPT-4 queries per malicious text; black-box crafting is essentially free (runtime ~1e-6 s), white-box ~26 s. It also transfers to a ReAct agent (ASR 0.72/0.58/0.52 on NQ/HotpotQA/MS-MARCO). Evaluated defenses are insufficient: paraphrasing only drops black-box NQ ASR from 0.97 to 0.87; perplexity detection is near-useless (ROC AUC 0.25 black-box, 0.30 white-box on NQ) because I is fluent GPT-4 text and S is a natural question; SHA-256 duplicate filtering leaves ASR unchanged (malicious texts are diverse); knowledge expansion (larger k) still leaves 41% (black-box)/43% (white-box) ASR on HotpotQA even at k=50 and gets worse as N grows.
core_contribution: PoisonedRAG is presented as the first knowledge-corruption (poisoning) attack targeting the knowledge database of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, rather than the LLM's or retriever's training data. The authors identify the RAG knowledge database as a new, practical attack surface and formulate the attack as a constrained optimization problem: craft a small set of malicious texts that, once injected into the database, make the RAG-backed LLM emit an attacker-chosen target answer for an attacker-chosen target question. Their central conceptual contribution is decomposing an effective attack into two necessary conditions - a 'retrieval condition' (the malicious text must be retrieved into the top-k for the target question) and a 'generation condition' (that text, used as context, must lead the LLM to produce the target answer). Each malicious text P is built as a concatenation of two sub-texts, P = S + I: I is LLM-generated (e.g., by GPT-4) to satisfy the generation condition, and S is crafted to satisfy the retrieval condition. In the black-box setting S is simply set to the target question Q itself (P = Q + I); in the white-box setting S is optimized against the retriever's parameters with an adversarial-text method (HotFlip) to maximize embedding similarity to Q.
threat_model: Attacker chooses M target questions and, for each, an arbitrary target answer (e.g., question 'Who is the CEO of OpenAI?', target answer 'Tim Cook'). The attacker's capability is to inject N malicious texts per target question into the knowledge database (e.g., by editing Wikipedia pages, posting fake news/websites, or as an enterprise insider); the paper cites prior work claiming ~6.5% of Wikipedia documents can be maliciously edited. The attacker CANNOT read existing texts in the knowledge database and CANNOT access or query the LLM. Two settings differ only by retriever knowledge: black-box (no access to or queries against the retriever - described as a strong/realistic threat model) and white-box (retriever parameters known, justified because RAG frameworks like NVIDIA ChatRTX default to a publicly available Hugging Face retriever, and by Kerckhoffs' principle). Attack goals include disinformation, commercially biased product recommendations, and financial disinformation. This attack is orthogonal to jailbreaking (which breaks safety alignment) and distinct from prompt injection (which injects instructions rather than knowledge).
limitations: By the authors' own scoping: evaluation centers on close-ended, factual QA (open-ended questions deferred to discussion) because they need answers that can be quantitatively scored via substring matching. Only 100 target questions per dataset (10 x 10 repetitions). The substring-matching ASR metric is acknowledged as imperfect (validated only against author-run human evaluation). The white-box setting assumes retriever parameters are known. Reviewer-noted gaps: the paper demonstrates attack feasibility and defeats several defenses but does not deliver an effective defense, explicitly ending with a call for new defenses; the injection-vector feasibility (e.g., 6.5% of Wikipedia being editable) is cited from prior work rather than demonstrated end-to-end; and generalization beyond QA (fact verification on FEVER) is only a limited extension rather than a full study.

datasets_used: Natural Questions (NQ), Wikipedia-based knowledge database with 2,681,468 texts
HotpotQA, Wikipedia-based knowledge database with 5,233,329 texts
MS-MARCO, Bing web-document knowledge database with 8,841,823 texts
FEVER (fact-verification extension in the discussion section)
quantitative_results: Default config injects N=5 malicious texts per target question, retrieves k=5 texts
PaLM 2 black-box ASR: NQ 0.97, HotpotQA 0.99, MS-MARCO 0.91
NQ black-box ASR across LLMs: PaLM 2 0.97, GPT-3.5 0.92, GPT-4 0.97, LLaMA-2-7B 0.97, LLaMA-2-13B 0.95, Vicuna-7B 0.88, Vicuna-13B 0.95, Vicuna-33B 0.91
NQ white-box ASR: GPT-3.5 0.99, GPT-4 0.99, PaLM 2 0.97; F1-Score 1.0
Efficiency: ~1.62 (NQ) to 2.69 (MS-MARCO) GPT-4 queries per malicious text; black-box runtime ~1.2-1.45e-6 s, white-box ~26 s per text
Perplexity detection ROC AUC on NQ: 0.25 (black-box), 0.30 (white-box)
Duplicate (SHA-256) filtering: ASR unchanged (e.g., NQ black-box stays 0.97)
Knowledge expansion at k=50 on HotpotQA: 41% (black-box), 43% (white-box) ASR still succeed
baselines_compared: Naive Attack (use question as malicious text): NQ ASR 0.03
Corpus Poisoning Attack (Zhong et al., random-character adversarial texts, white-box retriever): NQ ASR 0.01
Disinformation Attack (use crafted I alone, a PoisonedRAG variant): NQ ASR 0.69
Prompt Injection Attack (inject instruction with target question prepended): NQ ASR 0.62
GCG Attack (Zou et al., optimization-based adversarial suffix adapted to force target answer): NQ ASR 0.02
PoisonedRAG black-box 0.97 and white-box 0.97 on NQ default outperform all above
